question: Using Table 1.2-1, Table 5.1-2, and the RCS flow diagram Figure 5.1-3, describe the thermal-hydraulic operating conditions of the NuScale RCS.

nuscale_ch01_0072.title: Combined License Information Items
nuscale_ch01_0072.summary: This section outlines the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant certified design and the site-specific design provided in the combined license (COL) application. It explains the types of interface requirements (conceptual design information and COL information items), and describes how COL applicants must address site-specific information and any departures from the certified design. Tables referenced in the section summarize these interfaces and list the required COL information items.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes key components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. It removes heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation, and features systems for removing noncondensible gases and maintaining pressure boundary integrity to prevent radionuclide release. The RCS interfaces with several other systems, including emergency core cooling and chemical control systems, to ensure safe and efficient operation.

nuscale_ch01_0015.title: General Plant Description
nuscale_ch01_0015.summary: This section provides a summary of the NuScale plant’s overall design, including key systems such as safety features, power conversion, fuel handling, cooling, waste management, and auxiliary systems. It explains that each Combined License (COL) applicant must reference the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) and provide site-specific details where required, while distinguishing between certified design elements and conceptual design information (CDI). Security-related information is specially marked and withheld according to regulations.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance across different power levels by establishing steady-state parameters such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures, using both software and hand calculations. The evaluation considers uncertainties and design requirements to ensure efficient operation, maximize electrical generation, and maintain safety margins, with a focus on supporting natural circulation and minimizing pressure losses in the reactor coolant system.

nuscale_ch01_0069.title: Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams
nuscale_ch01_0069.summary: This section describes the use of simplified electrical, instrumentation and control (I&C), and mechanical drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information. It lists the relevant diagrams, figures, and legends used for both I&C and piping and instrumentation diagrams, and notes that site-specific diagrams and legends will be provided by COL applicants referencing the NuScale design certification.

nuscale_ch01_0059.title: Steam Generator Thermal-Hydraulic Performance Testing - Fluid-Heated Facility
nuscale_ch01_0059.summary: The electrically-heated steam generator test at the SIET facility used three instrumented tubes with controlled boundary conditions to simulate different heat flux regions (subcooled, boiling, superheat) and allowed precise measurement of thermal-hydraulic performance. The data collected supported evaluation of flow-induced vibration risks, informed the design of SG inlet flow restrictors, and provided benchmarking for NuScale’s design codes, with the program inspected by the NRC for compliance.

nuscale_ch01_0290.title: SRP BTP 8-9, Rev. 0: Open Phase Conditions in Electric Power SystemB.3 Criteria for evaluating open phase conditions for passive plant designs
nuscale_ch01_0290.summary: The section outlines NuScale's conformance with regulatory criteria regarding open phase conditions in electric power systems, specifically for passive plant designs. It states that the criteria in SRP BTP 8-9, Rev. 0 for passive plant designs are only partially met, with no specific exceptions noted, and references section 8.2.3 for further details. Additionally, it clarifies that requirements for active plant designs do not apply to passive designs.

nuscale_ch01_0058.title: Steam Generator Thermal-Hydraulic Performance Testing - Electrically Heated Facility
nuscale_ch01_0058.summary: This section describes the critical heat flux (CHF) testing conducted for the NuScale NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design using an electrically heated 5x5 simulated fuel bundle with AREVA HTP™/HMP™ grid technology. The tests, performed at the AREVA Karlstein Thermal Hydraulics facility, aimed to gather CHF data, validate CHF correlations, determine mixing coefficients, and measure pressure drop characteristics under various hydraulic conditions to support the NuScale Power Module (NPM) application.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat from the reactor after shutdown, especially when normal cooling is unavailable, without relying on external power. It ensures the reactor coolant system (RCS) temperature drops below 420°F within 36 hours, maintaining fuel and pressure boundary safety limits, and its reliability and risk significance are evaluated through established programs. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), but both can function together if needed.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the NuScale pressurizer, including the RPV upper shell, baffle plate, heater bundles, and associated components, specifying alloys and standards for corrosion resistance and compatibility. It references relevant sections of the BPVC and the Final Safety Analysis Report for further material specifications.

nuscale_ch01_0289.title: SRP BTP 8-9, Rev. 0: Open Phase Conditions in Electric Power SystemB.2 Criteria for evaluating open phase conditions for active plant designs
nuscale_ch01_0289.summary: The section states that the criteria for evaluating open phase conditions for active plant designs (SRP BTP 8-9, Rev. 0, Section B.2) are not applicable to passive plant designs like NuScale. Therefore, these regulatory requirements do not need to be addressed for the NuScale design.

nuscale_ch01_0118.title: 1.8 Interfaces with Certified Design
nuscale_ch01_0118.summary: This section explains the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant’s certified design and the site-specific design provided in the Combined License (COL) application. It distinguishes between conceptual design information (CDI) for non-certified plant portions and COL information items, which are site-specific responsibilities. The section also notes that COL applicants must address all COL information items and provide a list of any departures from the certified design.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator, ensuring safe and efficient operation. The RCS design supports up to twelve independent modules, maintains proper coolant chemistry and boron concentration, and is engineered to meet safety and performance requirements during normal and off-normal conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes the main components of the NuScale reactor system, focusing on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping, and the Pressurizer (PZR). The RPV serves as a primary barrier to fission product release and houses key reactor internals, while the RCS piping connects various subsystems and the PZR is integrated into the RPV to help control system pressure. Each component's functions and connections are briefly outlined, with references to further detailed sections.

nuscale_ch05_0014.title: Reactor Vessel
nuscale_ch05_0014.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module’s reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single vessel surrounded by a containment vessel. The RPV serves as a key pressure boundary to prevent the release of fission products and provides structural support for various reactor components. Materials used for the RPV are selected according to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code standards to ensure integrity and safety throughout the plant’s design life.

nuscale_ch01_0116.title: Figure 1.7-3e: Piping and Instrumentation Diagram Legends
nuscale_ch01_0116.summary: This section provides a comprehensive list of legends and abbreviations used in NuScale's Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs), detailing the various analysis functions, measurement types, and instrumentation identifiers. These legends standardize the symbols and codes for components such as sensors, analyzers, and measurement devices, ensuring clarity and consistency in interpreting the diagrams throughout the safety analysis report.

Section A discusses regulatory criteria for evaluating open phase conditions in the electric power system, specifically noting that NuScale's nonreliance on AC power makes certain outage time restrictions inapplicable, while Section B describes the design features of the NuScale RCS, including degasification and pressure boundary integrity, which are enabled by the system's design and operational independence from AC power as referenced in Section A.

Section B provides a general overview of the NuScale plant systems, including the RCS and its operating characteristics, which Section A references in the context of demonstrating conformance with regulatory criteria for electrical system design and safety-related functions.

Section A describes the testing methodology for steam generator thermal-hydraulic performance, while Section B details the materials and components of the pressurizer, with both sections referencing specific components and subsystems of the NuScale RCS that are relevant for understanding its thermal-hydraulic operating conditions.

Section B describes the Decay Heat Removal System's ability to remove decay heat and transition the reactor to safe shutdown without reliance on external power, which supports Section A's assertion that AC power source outage time restrictions are not applicable due to the plant's nonreliance on AC power for safety-related functions.

Section B provides detailed material specifications for the pressurizer components referenced in the general system design context of Section A, connecting the regulatory criteria for system reliability with the specific component design.

Section A explains that the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is designed to remove decay heat from the RCS without reliance on external (AC) power, while Section B states that NuScale's safety-related functions do not rely on AC power, making certain regulatory outage time restrictions inapplicable; thus, Section A's design feature directly supports Section B's regulatory justification.
